phrasal_verbs.meaning: practise and improve your skills or knowledge of something
change your opinion or decision because someone has persuaded you to agree with them
think of something such as an idea or a plan
accept something and try to deal with it
be able to understand something or solve a problem; understand what someone is like
suddenly have an idea; discover something by chance
see, hear or understand someone or something with difficulty; suggest, imply
think carefully about something over a period of time
learn the truth about something by considering all the separate bits of information
solve a confusing or complicated problem by thinking carefully about it
get information on a particular subject by reading a lot about it
study something very hard, especially for an examination
understand and remember something; accept something as real or true; trick someone
consider a problem or decision carefully
consider the facts about something in an organised and thorough way
invent or imagine something, especially an excuse

conclusion: come to / arrive at / reach a conclusion
perspective: from another / a different / sb’s / etc perspective
principle: stand by / stick to your principles
a matter / an issue of principle
question: beg the question
raise the question (of)
a/no question of
in question
out of the question
without question
beyond question
some question over / as to / about
awkward question

idioms.meaning: if success goes to your head, it makes you think that you are better or more important than you really are
be able to think quickly and make sensible decisions
not knowing very much about something, because other people are keeping it secret from you
know the important facts or the real situation

idioms.idiom: not have a leg to stand on
idioms.meaning: not have any way of proving that you are right about something

idioms.idiom: not see the wood for the trees
idioms.meaning: used for saying that someone cannot understand what is important because they are thinking too much about small details
guess what is happening or what something means based on what you have seen or heard
taking a very short/long time to understand or realise something
argue or worry about very small details or differences that are not important
spend some time thinking about the situation you are in before you decide what to do next

questions.sentence: Don’t be so optimistic / pessimistic. Everything’s going to be fine, you’ll see!
Most newspapers are usually biased / prejudiced towards a certain political outlook.
I’m slightly dubious / cynical about whether Sean’s idea is going to work.
To suggest that it’s possible to create a society without crime is a little naïve / sceptical, isn’t it?
The only ingenious / plausible explanation I can think of is that Jenna got the dates wrong.
Please don’t baffle / discriminate me with loads of technical terminology. Use plain, simple English!
It’s not always easy to assume / assess someone’s suitability for a job just by talking to them in a short interview.
How can the government justify / speculate spending so much on the armed forces?
Looking at the damage, I would estimate / deduce that the painting will cost at least €2,000 to restore properly.
I don’t think you should reflect / presume that lots of people are going to help you with this project.

B.title: Complete using the correct form of the words

questions.sentence: It took me quite a long time to ........................ the aim of the game was.
The jury are still ........................ , but they’re expected to reach a verdict soon.
I ........................ from your tutor that you’re thinking of changing course.
You really have to ........................ very hard when you are doing calligraphy.
I’m seriously ........................ asking for a pay rise tomorrow.
How long do you ........................ it’ll take to get to Manchester?
I don’t ........................ I could borrow your lecture notes this weekend, could I?
If you’ve ever ........................ the question ‘Why does the universe bother to exist?’, then this is the book for you.
Let’s not even ........................ the possibility of failure.

C.title: Correct the wrong words in bold

questions.sentence: I'm in a real hunch because I've been invited to two parties on Saturday and I don't know which one to go to.
Dave said he's got complete guesswork in us, and thinks we're going to do a great job.
Sarah's a/an intuition when it comes to astrophysics, but she's not very practical in everyday things.
You often need to use a little educated notion when you're starting a cryptic crossword.
I've got a/an ideology that someone in this office is going to get promoted soon.
Communism and capitalism are very different types of dilemma.
Who gave you the faith for the main character in the book?
Detective Tucker had absolutely no evidence that Roddard was involved, but all his paradox told him that he was.
I've got a small inspiration about the exam syllabus. Should I talk to Mrs Jacobs about it?
Paying mothers a salary to raise their children is an interesting genius.
The fact that more people would come here if the place wasn't so crowded is a real query.

matches.item: I'm going to have to mull this
matches.match: D over for a while before I make a final decision.

matches.item: Detectives are still trying to piece
matches.match: A together exactly what happened that evening.

matches.item: It took him a very long time to take
matches.match: B in the fact that he'd never see her again.
G upon such a great idea for a website.

matches.item: It took us ages to figure
matches.match: F out how to get everyone to the sports centre without Phil knowing.
C up a brilliant excuse to tell Rachel.
